question.text: What happens to option Greeks near expiry for an ATM option?

answer.text: Delta approaches 0.5, Gamma becomes very large (delta changes rapidly), Theta becomes very negative (rapid time decay), and Vega decreases (less time for volatility to matter)
All Greeks approach zero near expiry
Delta approaches 1, Gamma approaches 0, Theta approaches 0
All Greeks remain constant throughout the option's life

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Near expiry for ATM: Delta ≈ 0.5 (uncertain outcome). Gamma spikes (delta can flip rapidly between 0 and 1). Theta is very negative (time value evaporating rapidly). Vega decreases (less time for volatility to play out). This combination creates the 'gamma-theta tradeoff' — high gamma comes with high theta. **The Trap:** Greeks don't approach zero — they change dramatically near expiry. Delta doesn't approach 1 for ATM (it stays ≈ 0.5). Greeks are definitely NOT constant. **Cross-Exam Context:** This Greek behavior is identical for all ATM options across markets. It's why expiry-week trading is particularly volatile and risky. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The gamma spike near expiry makes ATM option writing extremely risky — a small move can cause the option to go from OTM to deep ITM rapidly. Many professional traders avoid writing ATM options in the last week.
